| Trusted Device Status | Filters sessions based on trusted device usage. | No | Must contain supported trusted device status values only. |
| Date From | Start date for session history retrieval where applicable. | No | Must not exceed the current system date and shall comply with configured retention policies. |
| Date To | End date for session history retrieval where applicable. | No | Must be greater than or equal to Date From and shall not exceed the current system date. |
| Time Zone | Customer-selected time zone for displaying session timestamps. | No | Must correspond to a supported system time zone. |
| Sort By | Specifies the attribute used for sorting session records. | No | Supported values include Login Time, Last Activity Time, Device Name, Session Status, or Authentication Method. |
| Sort Order | Specifies ascending or descending sorting order. | No | Allowed values: Ascending or Descending. |
| Page Number | Specifies the page number for paginated session results. | No | Must be a positive integer greater than zero. |
| Page Size | Specifies the number of session records returned per page. | No | Must remain within configured minimum and maximum pagination limits. |
| Confirmation Flag | Indicates customer confirmation before terminating one or more sessions where applicable. | No | Boolean value only. |
| Two-Factor Authentication (2FA) Verification Code | Verification code required for security-sensitive session management operations where applicable. | No | Must be valid, unexpired, and successfully verified. |
| Correlation ID | Unique request identifier for distributed tracing, diagnostics, and troubleshooting. | Yes | Must be unique for each request and generated according to platform standards. |

#### 3.1.31.6 Business Rules

1. Only authenticated customers shall be permitted to access the Session Management module.
2. Customers shall be permitted to view and manage only the sessions associated with their own customer account.
3. Every session management request shall be authenticated and authorized before processing.
4. The currently authenticated session shall be clearly identified within the session list.
5. Active sessions shall be displayed in reverse chronological order by default based on the most recent activity timestamp.
6. Each session shall display available information including device name, device type, operating system, browser or application, login timestamp, last activity timestamp, authentication method, session status, trusted device status where applicable, approximate location where permitted, and IP address where permitted by organizational privacy policies.
7. Customers shall be able to view detailed information for supported session records.
8. Customers shall be able to terminate individual active sessions.
9. Customers shall be able to terminate all active sessions except the currently authenticated session where supported.
10. The currently authenticated session shall not be terminated through the **Terminate All Other Sessions** operation.
11. Session termination shall immediately invalidate all authentication tokens associated with the terminated session.
12. Terminated sessions shall immediately lose access to all protected platform resources.
13. Session termination shall revoke all associated authorization privileges for the terminated session.
14. Session termination shall not affect other active sessions unless explicitly requested by the customer.
15. Customers shall be required to explicitly confirm security-sensitive session termination operations before execution.
16. Two-Factor Authentication (2FA) verification may be required for high-risk session management operations according to organizational security policies.
17. Fraud Detection Service shall evaluate session management requests for suspicious behavior where applicable.
18. Security Monitoring Service shall continuously monitor active sessions for abnormal activities and account takeover indicators.
19. High-risk session management operations may require additional identity verification according to configured organizational security policies.
20. Every successful session creation, renewal, timeout, expiration, revocation, and termination event shall be recorded.
21. Every successful and failed session management operation shall generate immutable audit records.
22. Historical session records shall remain immutable after creation.
23. Customers shall not be permitted to modify historical session records.
24. Historical session information shall be retained according to configured organizational retention policies.
25. Customers shall be able to search session information using supported search criteria.
26. Customers shall be able to filter session information using supported filter options.
27. Customers shall be able to sort session information using supported sorting options.
28. The module shall support configurable pagination for large session datasets.
29. Session timestamps shall be displayed using the customer's preferred time zone where configured.
30. Personally Identifiable Information (PII) shall be displayed only in accordance with applicable privacy regulations and organizational security policies.
31. Session information shall remain synchronized across Authentication Service, Authorization Service, Session Management Service, Device Management Service, Login Activity History Service, Two-Factor Authentication (2FA) Service, Security Monitoring Service, Fraud Detection Service, Customer Profile Service, Notification Service, Analytics Service, and Audit Logging Service.
32. Session-related notifications shall be generated according to configured customer notification preferences and organizational security policies.
33. All session management communications shall use secure HTTPS/TLS communication.
34. Operational metrics related to session management shall be collected for monitoring, reporting, analytics, auditing, capacity planning, and observability.
35. The Session Management module shall comply with organizational security policies, authentication standards, cybersecurity requirements, privacy regulations, audit requirements, consumer protection laws where applicable, accessibility standards where applicable, and all applicable legal and regulatory obligations.

#### 3.1.31.7 Validations

1. The customer shall be successfully authenticated before accessing the Session Management module.
2. The customer account shall exist and remain active.
3. The authenticated session shall be valid and unexpired.
4. The authentication token shall be valid and successfully verified.
5. Authorization Service shall verify that the customer is requesting access only to sessions associated with their own account.
6. Unauthorized attempts to access or manage another customer's sessions shall be rejected.
7. Customer permissions shall be successfully validated before processing any session management request.
8. The Session Management module shall be available.
9. Authentication Service shall be operational.
10. Authorization Service shall be operational.
11. Session Management Service shall be operational.
12. Device Management Service shall be available where device information is required.
13. Login Activity History Service shall be available where historical session information is requested.
14. Two-Factor Authentication (2FA) Service shall be operational where additional identity verification is required.
15. Security Monitoring Service shall be operational.
16. Fraud Detection Service shall be operational where risk evaluation is applicable.
17. Notification Service shall be operational where session-related notifications are generated.
18. Audit Logging Service shall be operational.
19. The requested Action Type shall contain only supported session management operations.
20. Target Session ID shall reference an existing session associated with the authenticated customer where applicable.
21. Target Session ID shall correspond to an active session when session termination is requested.
22. The currently authenticated session shall not be eligible for termination through the **Terminate All Other Sessions** operation.
23. Search keywords shall not exceed the configured maximum length.
24. Search keywords shall be sanitized to prevent SQL Injection, NoSQL Injection, Cross-Site Scripting (XSS), command injection, and other injection attacks.
25. Session Status filters shall contain only supported session status values.
26. Device Type filters shall contain only supported device categories.
27. Device Name filters shall reference only customer-associated devices where applicable.
28. Operating System filters shall contain supported operating system values.
29. Browser/Application filters shall contain supported browser or application values.
30. Authentication Method filters shall contain only supported authentication mechanisms.
31. Trusted Device Status filters shall contain supported trusted device status values.
32. Date From shall not exceed the current system date.
33. Date To shall not exceed the current system date.
34. Date To shall be greater than or equal to Date From.
35. Requested date ranges shall comply with configured session history retention policies.
36. Time Zone values shall correspond to supported system time zones.
37. Sort By values shall contain only supported sorting attributes.
38. Sort Order shall be limited to supported ascending or descending values.
39. Page Number shall be a positive integer greater than zero.
40. Page Size shall remain within configured minimum and maximum pagination limits.
41. Confirmation Flag shall contain only a valid Boolean value where applicable.
42. Two-Factor Authentication (2FA) verification codes shall be valid, unexpired, and successfully verified before protected operations are executed where required.
43. Correlation ID shall be present and unique for request tracing.
44. All requests shall be transmitted through secure HTTPS/TLS communication channels.
45. Rate limiting policies shall be enforced to prevent abusive or excessive session management requests.
46. Fraud Detection Service may require additional validation for suspicious session management activities.
47. Security Monitoring Service may perform additional behavioral analysis before permitting high-risk session operations.
48. Personally Identifiable Information (PII) shall be processed according to applicable privacy regulations and organizational security policies.
49. Every successful and failed session management request shall generate audit records where required by organizational security policies.
50. The request shall comply with organizational authentication policies, cybersecurity standards, privacy regulations, audit requirements, accessibility standards where applicable, consumer protection laws where applicable, and all applicable legal and regulatory obligations before processing.

#### 3.1.31.8 Main Flow

1. The authenticated customer navigates to the **Session Management** section from the Account Security settings or another supported entry point.
2. The system verifies that the customer's authentication session is valid and active.
3. The Authorization Service verifies that the customer is authorized to access only their own session information.
4. The system validates all incoming request parameters.
5. The system validates the requested session management action.
6. The system validates search, filtering, sorting, pagination, and date range parameters where applicable.
7. The system validates that the requested session belongs to the authenticated customer where a target session is specified.
8. The system validates organizational security policies applicable to the requested operation.
9. The system generates a unique Correlation ID for end-to-end request tracing if one is not already provided.
10. The Audit Logging Service records the session management request where required by organizational security policies.
11. The system retrieves all active sessions associated with the authenticated customer.
12. The system retrieves additional device information from the Device Management Service where applicable.
13. The system retrieves session-related authentication events from the Login Activity History Service where applicable.
14. The system retrieves authentication metadata from the Authentication Service where required.
15. The system retrieves fraud risk information from the Fraud Detection Service where applicable.
16. The system retrieves security event information from the Security Monitoring Service where applicable.
17. The system aggregates session information from integrated platform services.
18. The system removes duplicate or inconsistent session records according to configured data consistency policies.
19. The system enriches each session record with available metadata including device name, device type, operating system, browser/application, login timestamp, last activity timestamp, authentication method, trusted device status, approximate location where permitted, session status, and expiration details where applicable.
20. The system applies search criteria where specified.
21. The system applies filter criteria where specified.
22. The system applies sorting preferences.
23. The system applies pagination according to the requested page number and page size.
24. The system converts timestamps to the customer's preferred time zone where configured.
25. The system masks or omits sensitive information according to organizational privacy policies and applicable regulations.
26. If the requested action is **View Sessions**, the system prepares the session list for presentation.
27. If the requested action is **View Session Details**, the system retrieves and displays additional information for the selected session.
28. If the requested action is **Terminate Session**, the system requests customer confirmation where required.
29. Where organizational security policies require additional identity verification, the system performs Two-Factor Authentication (2FA) verification before executing the termination request.
30. The system validates that the selected session remains active before termination.
31. The Session Management Service terminates the selected session.
32. The Authentication Service immediately invalidates all authentication tokens associated with the terminated session.
33. The Authorization Service revokes all authorization privileges associated with the terminated session.
34. If the requested action is **Terminate All Other Sessions**, the system terminates every active session except the currently authenticated session.
35. Authentication tokens associated with all terminated sessions are immediately invalidated.
36. Notification Service generates applicable customer security notifications according to configured notification preferences.
37. Login Activity History Service records the completed session management event.
38. Audit Logging Service records the completed operation.
39. Operational metrics are updated for monitoring, analytics, reporting, observability, and capacity planning.
40. The system securely returns the updated session information and operation status to the authenticated customer.

#### 3.1.31.9 Alternate Flows

##### A. No Active Sessions Available

1. The authenticated customer accesses the Session Management module.
2. The system validates authentication and authorization.
3. The system determines that no active sessions exist for the customer account.
4. The system displays an informative message indicating that no active sessions are currently available.
5. The customer may refresh the page or return later.
6. The request completes successfully without generating an application error.

##### B. Search Returns No Matching Sessions

1. The customer enters search keywords or applies one or more filters.
2. The system validates the submitted search parameters.
3. The search operation is executed.
4. No session records match the specified criteria.
5. The system displays a "No matching sessions found" message.
6. The customer may modify or clear the search criteria and perform another search.

##### C. Invalid Search or Filter Criteria

1. The customer submits unsupported or invalid search parameters.
2. The system validates all input values.
3. One or more validation failures are identified.
4. The request is rejected.
5. Appropriate validation messages are displayed.
6. Previously displayed session information remains unchanged until a valid request is submitted.

##### D. Session Already Terminated

1. The customer selects an active session for termination.
2. Before execution, the Session Management Service determines that the selected session has already been terminated, expired, or revoked.
3. The requested termination operation is cancelled.
4. The system informs the customer that the selected session is no longer active.
5. The active session list is refreshed.
6. Audit records are generated where required.

##### E. Attempt to Terminate Current Session Using "Terminate All Other Sessions"

1. The customer requests **Terminate All Other Sessions**.
2. The system identifies the currently authenticated session.
3. All other active sessions are selected for termination.
4. The currently authenticated session is excluded automatically.
5. All eligible sessions are terminated successfully.
6. The current session remains active.

##### F. Two-Factor Authentication (2FA) Verification Failure

1. Additional identity verification is required according to organizational security policies.
2. The customer submits an invalid, expired, or unsuccessful 2FA verification.
3. The system rejects the protected session management operation.
4. No session termination occurs.
5. Appropriate security notifications and audit records are generated where applicable.
6. The customer may retry using a valid verification method.

##### G. Unauthorized Session Management Attempt

1. A request attempts to manage a session belonging to another customer.
2. Authorization Service validates session ownership.
3. Ownership verification fails.
4. The request is immediately rejected.
5. No session information is disclosed.
6. Security Monitoring Service and Audit Logging Service record the unauthorized access attempt.
7. Additional security controls may be triggered according to organizational security policies.

##### H. Fraud Detection Identifies High-Risk Activity

1. Fraud Detection Service identifies abnormal or suspicious session management behavior.
2. The request is evaluated according to configured risk policies.
3. Additional verification may be required.
4. High-risk operations may be temporarily blocked.
5. Security Monitoring Service records the event.
6. Administrative review and customer notifications may be initiated where applicable.

##### I. Dependent Service Temporarily Unavailable

1. One or more integrated platform services become temporarily unavailable.
2. The system performs configured retry operations.
3. Timeout, circuit breaker, fallback, or graceful degradation mechanisms are applied where supported.
4. If dependent services recover successfully, processing continues.
5. If recovery fails, an appropriate service unavailable response is returned.
6. Diagnostic and operational logs capture the failure details.

##### J. Internal System Failure

1. An unexpected internal system error occurs during session management processing.
2. The system safely terminates the current operation.
3. No partial or inconsistent session updates are committed.
4. A generic error response is returned without exposing sensitive implementation details.
5. Diagnostic information is recorded for operational investigation.
6. The customer may retry the operation after the issue has been resolved.

#### 3.1.31.10 Postconditions

1. The requested Session Management operation shall be successfully completed.
2. The authenticated customer shall be presented with only the session information associated with their own customer account.
3. No session information belonging to another customer shall be disclosed.
4. The current authentication session shall remain active unless the customer explicitly requests its termination through a supported operation.
5. Successfully terminated sessions shall immediately lose access to all protected platform resources.
6. Authentication tokens associated with terminated sessions shall be invalidated immediately.
7. Authorization privileges associated with terminated sessions shall be revoked immediately.
8. Active session status shall accurately reflect all completed session management operations.
9. Session lifecycle information shall remain synchronized with the Authentication Service.
10. Session status shall remain synchronized with the Session Management Service.
11. Device associations shall remain synchronized with the Device Management Service where applicable.
12. Session-related authentication events shall be synchronized with the Login Activity History Service.
13. Two-Factor Authentication (2FA) verification records shall be updated where applicable.
14. Security Monitoring Service shall record security-relevant session management events.
15. Fraud Detection Service shall update session risk evaluation information where applicable.
16. Customer Profile Service shall remain synchronized with current session information where applicable.
17. Notification Service shall generate applicable security notifications according to configured customer notification preferences.
18. Every successful session management operation shall generate immutable audit records.
19. Administrative session management activities shall remain fully traceable through immutable audit logs.
20. Historical session records shall remain immutable after creation.
21. Operational metrics related to session management shall be updated for monitoring, analytics, reporting, capacity planning, and observability.
22. Search, filtering, sorting, and pagination preferences shall be successfully applied where requested.
23. Session timestamps shall be presented using the customer's preferred time zone where configured.
24. Sensitive information shall remain masked or omitted according to organizational privacy policies and applicable regulations.
25. The authenticated customer's account settings shall remain unchanged unless explicitly modified through another authorized operation.
26. Personally Identifiable Information (PII) shall continue to be processed according to applicable privacy regulations and organizational security policies.
27. Integrated platform services shall remain synchronized across Authentication Service, Authorization Service, Session Management Service, Device Management Service, Login Activity History Service, Two-Factor Authentication (2FA) Service, Security Monitoring Service, Fraud Detection Service, Customer Profile Service, Notification Service, Analytics Service, and Audit Logging Service.
28. The Session Management module shall remain in a secure, consistent, available, recoverable, and auditable state following successful completion of the requested operation.
29. The completed operation shall comply with organizational security policies, authentication standards, cybersecurity requirements, privacy regulations, audit requirements, consumer protection laws where applicable, accessibility standards where applicable, and all applicable legal and regulatory obligations.
30. The customer may continue performing other permitted account security operations supported by the platform after completion of the session management request.

#### 3.1.31.11 Success Response

| Response Element | Description |
|------------------|-------------|
| Response Status | Indicates that the Session Management request completed successfully. |
| HTTP Status Code | **200 OK** or other appropriate success status based on the completed operation. |
| Status | SUCCESS |
| Response Code | SESSION_MANAGEMENT_SUCCESS |
| Message | Indicates that the requested session management operation has been completed successfully. |
| Customer ID | Unique identifier of the authenticated customer. |
| Action Type | Session management action successfully performed (View Sessions, View Session Details, Terminate Session, Terminate All Other Sessions, etc.). |
| Current Session ID | Unique identifier of the currently authenticated session where permitted by organizational security policies. |
| Target Session ID | Unique identifier of the affected session where applicable. |
| Active Session Count | Total number of active sessions remaining after completion of the requested operation. |
| Terminated Session Count | Total number of sessions successfully terminated where applicable. |
| Session List | Collection of active session records associated with the authenticated customer. |
| Session Status | Current status of each returned session (Active, Expired, Revoked, Logged Out, Timed Out, Terminated, etc.). |
| Device Information | Device name, device type, operating system, browser/application, and trusted device status where available. |
| Authentication Method | Authentication mechanism associated with each session. |
| Login Timestamp | Date and time when each session was created. |
| Last Activity Timestamp | Date and time of the most recent activity associated with each session. |
| Session Expiration Time | Scheduled expiration timestamp for active sessions where applicable. |
| Approximate Location | Approximate geographic location associated with the session where available and permitted by organizational privacy policies. |
| IP Address | Masked or permitted IP address information according to applicable privacy regulations and organizational security policies. |
| Notification Status | Indicates whether applicable session-related security notifications were successfully generated and delivered. |
| Audit Reference ID | Identifier of the generated audit record associated with the completed session management operation. |
| Correlation ID | Unique request identifier for distributed tracing, diagnostics, and troubleshooting. |
| Processed Timestamp | Date and time when the requested session management operation completed successfully. |
| Additional Information | Optional informational messages related to the completed session management operation. |

#### 3.1.31.12 Failure Response

| Response Element | Description |
|------------------|-------------|
| Response Status | Indicates that the Session Management request failed. |
| HTTP Status Code | Appropriate error status such as **400 Bad Request**, **401 Unauthorized**, **403 Forbidden**, **404 Not Found**, **408 Request Timeout**, **409 Conflict**, **422 Unprocessable Entity**, **429 Too Many Requests**, **500 Internal Server Error**, **502 Bad Gateway**, or **503 Service Unavailable**. |
| Status | FAILED |
| Response Code | System-defined error code representing the reason for failure. |
| Error Message | Human-readable description of the failure. |
| Failure Reason | Detailed explanation of why the requested session management operation could not be completed. |
| Customer ID | Authenticated customer identifier where available. |
| Action Type | Session management action that failed (View Sessions, View Session Details, Terminate Session, Terminate All Other Sessions, etc.). |
| Target Session ID | Identifier of the affected session where applicable. |
| Authentication Status | Indicates whether authentication validation failed. |
| Authorization Status | Indicates whether authorization validation failed. |
| Session Status | Indicates whether the requested session is invalid, expired, revoked, already terminated, or unavailable where applicable. |
| Two-Factor Authentication (2FA) Status | Indicates whether required 2FA verification failed where applicable. |
| Security Validation Status | Indicates whether organizational security policy validation failed. |
| Fraud Detection Status | Indicates whether fraud detection prevented execution of the requested operation. |
| Search Validation Status | Indicates whether submitted search parameters failed validation where applicable. |
| Filter Validation Status | Indicates whether one or more filtering criteria are invalid where applicable. |
| Date Range Validation Status | Indicates whether the requested date range violates configured validation or retention policies where applicable. |
| Pagination Validation Status | Indicates whether pagination parameters are invalid where applicable. |
| Validation Errors | Collection of field-level validation errors where applicable. |
| Dependency Service Status | Indicates whether one or more integrated platform services are unavailable or failed during processing. |
| Retry Allowed | Indicates whether the request may be safely retried. |
| Retry After | Suggested retry duration where applicable. |
| Timestamp | Date and time when the failure occurred. |
| Audit Reference ID | Identifier of the generated audit record where applicable. |
| Correlation ID | Unique request identifier for distributed tracing, diagnostics, and troubleshooting. |
| Support Reference | Reference identifier for customer support and operational investigations where applicable. |
| Additional Information | Optional diagnostic information that does not expose sensitive authentication details, session secrets, internal implementation information, confidential security controls, or Personally Identifiable Information (PII). |

#### 3.1.31.13 Non-Functional Considerations

1. The Session Management module shall maintain **99.9% or higher availability**, excluding planned maintenance windows.
2. The module shall support horizontal and vertical scalability without service interruption.
3. The system shall support concurrent session management requests from millions of authenticated customers.
4. Session retrieval, validation, and termination operations shall maintain acceptable response times under expected production workloads and peak traffic conditions.
5. The module shall efficiently support large numbers of active and historical sessions through configurable pagination.
6. Session information shall be indexed to enable efficient searching, filtering, sorting, and retrieval.
7. All communications between clients and platform services shall occur exclusively over secure HTTPS/TLS connections.
8. Session identifiers, authentication tokens, refresh tokens, cryptographic secrets, passwords, OTP values, and other sensitive authentication information shall never be exposed through customer-facing interfaces.
9. Session identifiers displayed to customers shall be masked or partially displayed where required by organizational security policies.
10. Authentication and refresh tokens associated with terminated sessions shall be invalidated immediately after successful session termination.
11. Session information shall be protected against unauthorized access, disclosure, modification, replay attacks, session hijacking, and session fixation attacks.
12. Personally Identifiable Information (PII) associated with session information shall be processed according to applicable privacy regulations and organizational security policies.
13. Every successful and failed session management operation shall generate immutable audit records.
14. Audit records shall remain tamper-resistant and retained according to configured organizational retention policies.
15. Every request shall include a Correlation ID to support distributed tracing, diagnostics, and troubleshooting.
16. Session information shall remain synchronized across Authentication Service, Authorization Service, Session Management Service, Device Management Service, Login Activity History Service, Two-Factor Authentication (2FA) Service, Security Monitoring Service, Fraud Detection Service, Customer Profile Service, Notification Service, Analytics Service, and Audit Logging Service.
17. Temporary failures of dependent services shall be handled using configurable retry mechanisms, timeout policies, circuit breakers, fallback strategies, and graceful degradation where applicable.
18. Session management operations shall remain resilient against infrastructure failures and partial service outages.
19. Database operations supporting session lifecycle management shall maintain transactional consistency.
20. Session termination requests shall support idempotent processing where applicable to prevent duplicate termination operations.
21. Business rules governing session lifecycle, timeout policies, concurrent sessions, and security controls shall be configurable without requiring application redeployment wherever possible.
22. Operational metrics shall be collected for monitoring, reporting, analytics, capacity planning, performance optimization, and observability.
23. Security Monitoring Service shall continuously monitor abnormal session behavior and account takeover attempts.
24. Fraud Detection Service shall evaluate suspicious session management requests according to configured organizational risk policies.
25. Configurable rate limiting, abuse prevention, anomaly detection, bot protection, and denial-of-service mitigation mechanisms shall protect Session Management APIs.
26. Customer-facing interfaces shall support localization and internationalization.
27. Accessibility requirements shall comply with applicable accessibility standards such as WCAG where required.
28. Backup and disaster recovery mechanisms shall protect session-related metadata, historical session records, and audit information.
29. Recovery procedures shall satisfy configured Recovery Time Objectives (RTO) and Recovery Point Objectives (RPO).
30. Historical session records shall be retained according to configured retention policies and applicable legal or regulatory requirements.
31. Administrative access to session information shall be governed by Role-Based Access Control (RBAC) and remain fully traceable.
32. The Session Management module shall comply with organizational security policies, authentication standards, ISO 27001 security controls where adopted, OWASP security recommendations, privacy regulations, cybersecurity requirements, consumer protection laws where applicable, accessibility standards where applicable, audit requirements, and all applicable legal and regulatory obligations.
33. The module shall maintain confidentiality, integrity, availability, auditability, consistency, and recoverability of session information throughout the complete session lifecycle.

#### 3.1.31.14 Acceptance Criteria

1. Only authenticated customers shall be able to access the Session Management module.
2. Customers shall be able to view and manage only the sessions associated with their own customer account.
3. Unauthorized users shall not be able to access or manage another customer's sessions.
4. Every session management request shall be authenticated and authorized before processing.
5. Active sessions shall be displayed in reverse chronological order by default based on the most recent activity.
6. The currently authenticated session shall be clearly identified in the session list.
7. Customers shall be able to view supported session details including device information, authentication method, login timestamp, last activity timestamp, session status, and expiration details where applicable.
8. Customers shall be able to terminate an individual active session.
9. Customers shall be able to terminate all active sessions except the currently authenticated session where supported.
10. The currently authenticated session shall remain active when the **Terminate All Other Sessions** operation is executed.
11. Successfully terminated sessions shall immediately lose access to protected platform resources.
12. Authentication tokens associated with terminated sessions shall be invalidated immediately.
13. Authorization privileges associated with terminated sessions shall be revoked immediately.
14. Session status shall accurately reflect completed session lifecycle operations.
15. Customers shall be able to search session information using supported search criteria.
16. Customers shall be able to filter session information using supported filter options.
17. Customers shall be able to sort session information using supported sorting options.
18. The module shall support configurable pagination for large session datasets.
19. Two-Factor Authentication (2FA) verification shall be enforced for protected session management operations where required by organizational security policies.
20. Fraud Detection Service shall evaluate suspicious session management activities according to configured risk policies.
21. Security Monitoring Service shall monitor abnormal session behavior and account takeover indicators.
22. Historical session records shall remain immutable after creation.
23. Historical session records shall be retained according to configured organizational retention policies.
24. Every successful and failed session management operation shall generate immutable audit records where required by organizational security policies.
25. Administrative session management operations shall require Role-Based Access Control (RBAC).
26. Administrative access shall remain fully auditable.
27. Session information shall remain synchronized across Authentication Service, Authorization Service, Session Management Service, Device Management Service, Login Activity History Service, Two-Factor Authentication (2FA) Service, Security Monitoring Service, Fraud Detection Service, Customer Profile Service, Notification Service, Analytics Service, and Audit Logging Service.
28. Session-related notifications shall be generated according to configured notification preferences and organizational security policies.
29. All Session Management communications shall use secure HTTPS/TLS encryption.
30. Sensitive authentication information including passwords, OTP values, authentication tokens, refresh tokens, session secrets, and cryptographic credentials shall never be exposed through customer-facing interfaces.
31. Personally Identifiable Information (PII) shall be processed according to applicable privacy regulations and organizational security policies.
32. Operational metrics shall be updated for monitoring, reporting, analytics, observability, performance optimization, and capacity planning.
33. The Session Management module shall remain available under expected production workloads without unacceptable performance degradation.
34. The Session Management module shall comply with organizational security policies, authentication standards, cybersecurity requirements, privacy regulations, audit requirements, consumer protection laws where applicable, accessibility standards where applicable, and all applicable legal and regulatory obligations.


### 3.1.32 Two-Factor Authentication (2FA)

#### 3.1.32.1 Description

The Two-Factor Authentication (2FA) module provides an additional layer of security beyond username/password authentication by requiring customers to successfully complete a second verification step before access is granted to protected resources or sensitive account operations.

The primary objective of this module is to reduce the risk of unauthorized account access resulting from compromised passwords, phishing attacks, credential stuffing, brute-force attacks, session hijacking, or stolen authentication credentials.

The platform shall support configurable Two-Factor Authentication methods, allowing customers to verify their identity using one or more supported authentication factors based on organizational security policies.

Supported authentication methods may include:

- One-Time Password (OTP) via SMS
- One-Time Password (OTP) via Email
- Time-Based One-Time Password (TOTP) using Authenticator Applications
- Push Notification Approval
- Backup Recovery Codes
- Hardware Security Keys (Future Enhancement)

The platform shall allow administrators to configure whether Two-Factor Authentication is:

- Optional
- Recommended
- Mandatory
- Risk-Based
- Enabled only for privileged operations

Customers may be required to complete Two-Factor Authentication during scenarios including but not limited to:

- Login from a new device
- Login from an unrecognized browser
- Login from an unfamiliar geographic location
- Login after password reset
- Login after prolonged inactivity
- Login after suspicious activity detection
- Login from high-risk IP addresses
- Accessing Privacy Settings
- Updating Contact Information
- Changing Password
- Managing Saved Payment Methods
- Viewing Wallet Information
- Managing Session Settings
- Deleting Customer Account
- Performing other security-sensitive operations

The module shall integrate with:

- Authentication Service
- Authorization Service
- Identity Management Service
- Device Management Service
- Session Management Service
- Login Activity History Service
- Notification Service
- Fraud Detection Service
- Security Monitoring Service
- Customer Profile Service
- Analytics Service
- Audit Logging Service

Every Two-Factor Authentication event shall be securely logged for compliance, security monitoring, analytics, fraud investigation, and audit purposes.

---

#### 3.1.32.2 Actors

**Primary Actors**

- Customer
- Registered Customer

**Secondary Actors**

- Authentication Service
- Authorization Service
- Identity Management Service
- Notification Service
- SMS Gateway
- Email Service
- Authenticator Application
- Push Notification Service
- Device Management Service
- Session Management Service
- Login Activity History Service
- Fraud Detection Service
- Security Monitoring Service
- Customer Profile Service
- Analytics Service
- Audit Logging Service
- System Administrator

---

#### 3.1.32.3 Preconditions

1. Customer account shall exist.
2. Customer account shall be active.
3. Customer shall have successfully completed primary authentication where applicable.
4. Customer credentials shall be successfully validated before initiating second-factor verification.
5. Customer shall have at least one supported Two-Factor Authentication method configured when mandatory.
6. Authentication Service shall be operational.
7. Authorization Service shall be operational.
8. Notification Service shall be operational for SMS, Email, or Push-based authentication methods.
9. SMS Gateway shall be available when SMS OTP is selected.
10. Email Service shall be available when Email OTP is selected.
11. Authenticator Application shall be properly configured when TOTP is selected.
12. Push Notification Service shall be operational for Push Authentication.
13. Customer device shall have internet connectivity where required.
14. System clocks shall remain synchronized for TOTP verification.
15. Customer account shall not be locked.
16. Customer shall not exceed configured verification attempt limits.
17. Fraud Detection Service shall complete risk assessment where applicable.
18. Security Monitoring Service shall be operational.
19. Audit Logging Service shall be available.
20. Customer profile shall contain verified contact information where required.
21. Device Management Service shall identify whether login originates from a trusted device.
22. Session Management Service shall create a pending authentication session before successful 2FA completion.

---

#### 3.1.32.4 Trigger

The Two-Factor Authentication process may be initiated when one or more of the following events occur:

1. Customer logs in from a new device.
2. Customer logs in from an unknown browser.
3. Customer logs in from an unrecognized location.
4. Customer logs in using high-risk IP addresses.
5. Customer attempts to change password.
6. Customer updates registered mobile number.
7. Customer updates registered email address.
8. Customer modifies privacy settings.
9. Customer manages saved payment methods.
10. Customer accesses customer wallet.
11. Customer deletes saved devices.
12. Customer terminates all active sessions.
13. Customer requests sensitive account information.
14. Customer performs high-value financial operations.
15. Fraud Detection Service identifies suspicious login behavior.
16. Security Monitoring Service identifies abnormal authentication patterns.
17. Administrator enables mandatory Two-Factor Authentication.
18. Organizational security policy requires identity verification.
19. Customer manually enables Two-Factor Authentication.
20. Customer disables Two-Factor Authentication.
21. Risk-based authentication policies require secondary verification.
22. Platform security configuration initiates mandatory identity verification.

---

#### 3.1.32.5 Input Fields

| Field | Description | Mandatory |
|--------|-------------|-----------|
| Authentication Method | Selected verification method | Yes |
| OTP Code | One-Time Password received by customer | Conditional |
| Authenticator Code | TOTP generated by authenticator application | Conditional |
| Push Approval Status | User approval from push notification | Conditional |
| Backup Recovery Code | Emergency recovery code | Conditional |
| Device Identifier | Unique device identifier | Yes |
| Browser Information | Browser metadata | Yes |
| Operating System | Client operating system | Yes |
| Device Name | Friendly device name | No |
| IP Address | Client IP address | System Generated |
| Location Information | Estimated login location | System Generated |
| Correlation ID | Request tracking identifier | System Generated |
| Session ID | Pending authentication session | System Generated |
| Authentication Timestamp | Verification timestamp | System Generated |
| Risk Score | Fraud assessment score | System Generated |

---

#### 3.1.32.6 Business Rules

1. Two-Factor Authentication shall always occur after successful primary credential validation.
2. Authentication shall not complete until second-factor verification succeeds.
3. Failed primary authentication shall prevent initiation of Two-Factor Authentication.
4. Every OTP shall be cryptographically secure.
5. OTP values shall be randomly generated.
6. OTP validity duration shall be configurable.
7. Expired OTPs shall become permanently invalid.
8. Used OTPs shall never be reusable.
9. OTP reuse shall be rejected.
10. Customers shall not receive multiple simultaneously active OTPs for the same authentication request unless explicitly configured.
11. Generating a new OTP may invalidate previously generated OTPs according to security policy.
12. Maximum OTP resend attempts shall be configurable.
13. Maximum OTP verification attempts shall be configurable.
14. Exceeding verification attempts may temporarily block authentication.
15. Repeated failures may trigger account protection mechanisms.
16. Risk-based authentication policies may require additional verification steps.
17. Customers may choose from supported authentication methods when multiple methods are available.
18. Mandatory authentication methods shall override customer preferences where required.
19. Trusted devices may bypass Two-Factor Authentication according to configured organizational policies.
20. Trust duration for remembered devices shall be configurable.
21. High-risk logins shall always require Two-Factor Authentication regardless of trusted device status.
22. Fraud Detection Service may require additional verification before granting access.
23. Security Monitoring Service may terminate authentication upon detecting malicious behavior.
24. Successful authentication shall immediately create an authenticated session.
25. Failed authentication shall never create authenticated sessions.
26. Every authentication attempt shall be recorded in Login Activity History.
27. Every authentication event shall generate immutable audit logs.
28. Notifications shall be sent for successful and failed Two-Factor Authentication events according to notification preferences and security policies.
29. Administrative overrides shall require elevated privileges and complete audit logging.
30. Backup recovery codes shall be single-use only.
31. Customers shall be able to regenerate backup recovery codes after successful authentication.
32. Previously generated recovery codes shall become invalid after regeneration.
33. System administrators shall not be able to view customer OTP values.
34. Sensitive authentication secrets shall never be stored in plaintext.
35. TOTP shared secrets shall be encrypted at rest.
36. Authentication decisions shall comply with organizational cybersecurity policies.
37. Authentication records shall remain available for compliance, investigations, analytics, and auditing.
38. The module shall support future extensibility for biometric authentication and hardware security keys without significant architectural changes.


#### 3.1.32.7 Validations

1. Customer account shall exist before initiating Two-Factor Authentication.
2. Customer account shall be active.
3. Customer account shall not be suspended.
4. Customer account shall not be permanently blocked.
5. Primary authentication shall be successfully completed before second-factor verification begins.
6. Selected authentication method shall be supported by the platform.
7. Selected authentication method shall be enabled for the customer.
8. Mandatory authentication methods shall not be bypassed.
9. OTP shall not be null.
10. OTP shall not be empty.
11. OTP shall contain only valid characters.
12. OTP length shall match configured policy.
13. OTP shall not be expired.
14. OTP shall belong to the current authentication session.
15. OTP shall not have been previously used.
16. OTP shall be cryptographically verified before authentication succeeds.
17. TOTP verification shall consider acceptable clock drift based on configured tolerance.
18. TOTP verification shall fail if the shared secret is invalid.
19. Backup recovery code shall exist.
20. Backup recovery code shall not have been previously consumed.
21. Backup recovery code shall immediately become invalid after successful usage.
22. Authentication request shall belong to the authenticated customer.
23. Device identifier shall be valid.
24. Device information shall match current authentication request.
25. Pending authentication session shall exist.
26. Pending authentication session shall not be expired.
27. Pending authentication session shall not be completed.
28. Pending authentication session shall not be cancelled.
29. Pending authentication session shall not already be authenticated.
30. Verification attempts shall not exceed configured maximum limits.
31. OTP resend requests shall not exceed configured resend limits.
32. Resend requests shall comply with rate-limiting policies.
33. Authentication requests shall comply with API rate limits.
34. Customer IP address shall pass security validation.
35. High-risk IP addresses shall be evaluated according to fraud policies.
36. Browser fingerprint shall be validated where applicable.
37. Device trust status shall be verified before bypassing Two-Factor Authentication.
38. Fraud Detection Service shall evaluate suspicious authentication attempts.
39. Security Monitoring Service shall validate behavioral anomalies.
40. Correlation ID shall be generated for every authentication request.
41. Required audit information shall be available before completing authentication.
42. Authentication responses shall never expose internal security implementation details.
43. Sensitive authentication information shall never appear in API responses.
44. Authentication tokens shall only be generated after successful Two-Factor Authentication.
45. Every validation failure shall return an appropriate business error without exposing confidential security information.

---

#### 3.1.32.8 Main Flow

1. Customer submits valid username, email, or mobile number together with password.
2. Authentication Service validates the primary credentials.
3. System verifies that the customer account is active and eligible for authentication.
4. Fraud Detection Service performs initial risk assessment.
5. Security Monitoring Service evaluates authentication behavior.
6. Platform determines whether Two-Factor Authentication is required.
7. System identifies available authentication methods configured for the customer.
8. Platform determines mandatory authentication method based on organizational security policies.
9. Customer selects preferred authentication method when multiple methods are permitted.
10. System creates a pending authentication session.
11. Correlation ID is generated for request tracking.
12. OTP or authentication challenge is securely generated.
13. Generated authentication challenge is securely associated with the pending session.
14. Authentication challenge expiration timestamp is established.
15. Authentication challenge is securely delivered using the selected communication channel.
16. Platform displays verification screen.
17. Customer enters the received OTP or authentication code.
18. System validates authentication session.
19. System validates authentication challenge expiration.
20. System verifies verification attempt limits.
21. Platform cryptographically validates the submitted authentication code.
22. Fraud Detection Service performs secondary risk evaluation.
23. Security Monitoring Service validates authentication behavior.
24. Authentication Service confirms successful second-factor verification.
25. Pending authentication session is upgraded to an authenticated session.
26. Authentication tokens are securely generated.
27. Refresh tokens are securely generated where applicable.
28. Session Management Service creates the authenticated customer session.
29. Device Management Service updates trusted device information where applicable.
30. Login Activity History records the successful authentication.
31. Audit Logging Service records the complete authentication event.
32. Analytics Service records authentication metrics.
33. Notification Service sends successful login notification according to configured preferences.
34. Customer is redirected to the requested application resource.
35. Authentication process completes successfully.

---

#### 3.1.32.9 Alternate Flows

##### AF-1: Invalid OTP

1. Customer enters an incorrect OTP.
2. Platform rejects the verification attempt.
3. Remaining verification attempts are updated.
4. Customer is informed that the verification code is invalid.
5. Customer is allowed to retry if attempts remain.
6. Authentication remains pending.

---

##### AF-2: OTP Expired

1. Customer submits an expired OTP.
2. Platform identifies the OTP as expired.
3. Verification request is rejected.
4. Customer is informed that the OTP has expired.
5. Customer is allowed to request a new OTP.
6. Previous OTP becomes permanently invalid.

---

##### AF-3: Maximum Verification Attempts Exceeded

1. Customer repeatedly enters incorrect authentication codes.
2. Verification attempt counter reaches configured maximum.
3. Platform blocks further verification attempts.
4. Authentication request is cancelled.
5. Customer receives security notification.
6. Audit records are created.
7. Fraud Detection Service is notified.
8. Security Monitoring Service records the event.

---

##### AF-4: OTP Resend Requested

1. Customer selects **Resend OTP**.
2. Platform validates resend eligibility.
3. Rate-limiting rules are evaluated.
4. Previous OTP may be invalidated according to policy.
5. New OTP is generated.
6. OTP expiration time is reset.
7. New OTP is securely delivered.
8. Customer continues verification.

---

##### AF-5: Authenticator Application Verification

1. Customer selects Authenticator App verification.
2. Platform displays verification screen.
3. Customer opens authenticator application.
4. Customer enters generated TOTP.
5. Platform validates the generated code.
6. Authentication succeeds.
7. Customer gains account access.

---

##### AF-6: Push Notification Authentication

1. Platform sends push authentication request.
2. Customer receives push notification.
3. Customer reviews login request.
4. Customer approves authentication.
5. Push service returns successful approval.
6. Authentication completes successfully.

---

##### AF-7: Push Notification Rejected

1. Customer rejects push notification.
2. Authentication immediately fails.
3. Pending session is terminated.
4. Customer remains unauthenticated.
5. Security event is logged.
6. Customer receives login denial notification.

---

##### AF-8: Backup Recovery Code Used

1. Customer cannot access normal authentication method.
2. Customer selects backup recovery option.
3. Backup recovery code is entered.
4. Platform validates recovery code.
5. Recovery code is marked as consumed.
6. Authentication succeeds.
7. Customer is advised to regenerate backup codes.

---

##### AF-9: Trusted Device Login

1. Customer logs in from a trusted device.
2. Organizational policy allows trusted-device bypass.
3. Fraud Detection Service confirms low risk.
4. Platform skips second-factor verification.
5. Authenticated session is created.
6. Login Activity History records trusted-device authentication.

---

##### AF-10: High-Risk Login Detected

1. Customer attempts login from an unfamiliar location or suspicious IP.
2. Fraud Detection Service assigns high-risk score.
3. Platform ignores trusted-device status.
4. Mandatory Two-Factor Authentication is enforced.
5. Additional security monitoring begins.
6. Authentication proceeds only after successful verification.

---

##### AF-11: Notification Service Failure

1. OTP generation succeeds.
2. Notification Service fails to deliver OTP.
3. Platform retries delivery according to retry policy.
4. If retries fail, customer is informed of temporary service unavailability.
5. Authentication remains pending.
6. Audit log is recorded.

---

##### AF-12: SMS Gateway Failure

1. SMS Gateway becomes unavailable.
2. SMS delivery fails.
3. Platform attempts configured retries.
4. If alternative authentication methods are available, customer is prompted to choose another method.
5. Authentication continues using the newly selected method.
6. SMS failure is logged.

---

##### AF-13: Email Delivery Failure

1. Email OTP delivery fails.
2. Platform retries email delivery.
3. Customer may switch to another available authentication method.
4. Previous OTP is invalidated if required.
5. New verification request is generated.
6. Authentication continues.

---

##### AF-14: Fraud Detection Blocks Authentication

1. Fraud Detection Service identifies severe security risk.
2. Authentication request is immediately blocked.
3. Customer account may be temporarily protected.
4. Security Monitoring Service receives alert.
5. Administrator notification may be generated.
6. Customer is shown an appropriate security message without exposing internal risk assessment details.


##### AF-15: Security Monitoring Service Blocks Authentication

1. Security Monitoring Service detects abnormal authentication behavior.
2. The authentication request is flagged as suspicious.
3. The pending authentication session is temporarily suspended.
4. Fraud Detection Service performs additional risk evaluation.
5. Customer may be required to complete additional identity verification.
6. Authentication proceeds only after successful security validation.
7. Complete security audit logs are generated.

---

##### AF-16: Authentication Session Expired

1. Customer delays verification beyond the configured authentication timeout.
2. The pending authentication session expires automatically.
3. The submitted verification code is rejected.
4. Customer is informed that the authentication session has expired.
5. Customer is required to initiate a new login request.
6. Expired authentication session is archived for audit purposes.

---

##### AF-17: Customer Cancels Authentication

1. Customer selects **Cancel Authentication**.
2. The pending authentication session is terminated.
3. Generated authentication challenges become invalid.
4. Temporary authentication data is securely removed.
5. Audit logs are created.
6. Customer returns to the login page.

---

##### AF-18: Account Locked During Authentication

1. Customer account becomes locked due to organizational security policies.
2. Pending authentication immediately stops.
3. Generated authentication challenges become invalid.
4. Customer is informed that the account has been temporarily locked.
5. Security notifications are generated.
6. Authentication request is terminated.

---

##### AF-19: System Maintenance

1. Authentication request is received during scheduled maintenance.
2. Platform detects maintenance mode.
3. New authentication requests are temporarily suspended.
4. Customer receives a maintenance notification.
5. Authentication is not initiated.
6. Audit logs are generated.

---

##### AF-20: Internal System Failure

1. Unexpected internal application failure occurs.
2. Authentication process is safely aborted.
3. No authenticated session is created.
4. Sensitive authentication data is securely discarded.
5. Technical logs are recorded for investigation.
6. Customer receives a generic error message without revealing internal implementation details.

---

#### 3.1.32.10 Postconditions

##### Successful Completion

1. Customer identity is successfully verified using the configured second authentication factor.
2. Authenticated customer session is created.
3. Authentication tokens are securely generated.
4. Refresh tokens are generated where applicable.
5. Pending authentication session is converted into an authenticated session.
6. Login Activity History is updated.
7. Device Management Service updates trusted device information where applicable.
8. Fraud Detection Service records authentication outcome.
9. Security Monitoring Service updates behavioral analytics.
10. Customer Profile Service updates the last successful authentication timestamp.
11. Notification Service sends successful login notifications according to configured preferences.
12. Analytics Service records authentication metrics.
13. Immutable audit logs are created.
14. Customer gains access to authorized platform resources.

##### Failed Completion

1. No authenticated session is created.
2. Authentication tokens are not generated.
3. Authorization to protected resources is denied.
4. Pending authentication session is terminated or expires according to policy.
5. Failed authentication attempt is recorded.
6. Verification attempt counters are updated.
7. Fraud Detection Service updates customer risk indicators.
8. Security Monitoring Service records suspicious activities where applicable.
9. Login Activity History records failed authentication.
10. Audit logs are generated.
11. Customer receives an appropriate failure response.
12. Platform remains in a secure state.

---

#### 3.1.32.11 Success Response

Upon successful completion of Two-Factor Authentication, the platform shall:

1. Display a confirmation indicating successful identity verification.
2. Create an authenticated customer session.
3. Generate secure authentication tokens.
4. Generate refresh tokens where applicable.
5. Redirect the customer to the originally requested application resource.
6. Display customer dashboard or requested protected page.
7. Update trusted device information where applicable.
8. Record successful authentication in Login Activity History.
9. Generate immutable audit records.
10. Update authentication analytics.
11. Trigger configured security notifications.
12. Synchronize authentication status across Authentication Service, Authorization Service, Session Management Service, Device Management Service, Login Activity History Service, Security Monitoring Service, Fraud Detection Service, Customer Profile Service, Analytics Service, Notification Service, and Audit Logging Service.

---

#### 3.1.32.12 Failure Response

If Two-Factor Authentication cannot be completed successfully, the platform shall:

1. Reject the authentication request.
2. Prevent creation of an authenticated session.
3. Prevent generation of authentication tokens.
4. Prevent access to protected platform resources.
5. Display an appropriate customer-friendly error message without exposing internal security implementation details.
6. Record failure reason for audit purposes.
7. Increment verification attempt counters where applicable.
8. Update Login Activity History.
9. Notify Fraud Detection Service where suspicious behavior is identified.
10. Notify Security Monitoring Service where abnormal behavior is detected.
11. Generate immutable audit records.
12. Trigger security notifications according to configured organizational policies.
13. Continue enforcing rate-limiting and abuse prevention mechanisms.
14. Lock or temporarily restrict authentication after repeated failures according to configured security policies.
15. Require a new authentication request if the current authentication session expires or becomes invalid.

---

#### 3.1.32.13 Non-Functional Considerations

1. The Two-Factor Authentication module shall maintain **99.9% or higher availability**, excluding scheduled maintenance.
2. The module shall support millions of concurrent authentication requests without unacceptable performance degradation.
3. OTP generation, delivery, validation, and authentication workflows shall complete within acceptable production response times.
4. The module shall support horizontal and vertical scalability.
5. All authentication communications shall use HTTPS/TLS encryption.
6. OTP values shall be generated using cryptographically secure random number generators.
7. TOTP shared secrets shall remain encrypted at rest.
8. Authentication secrets shall never be stored or transmitted in plaintext.
9. Authentication APIs shall support configurable rate limiting.
10. The module shall protect against brute-force attacks, replay attacks, credential stuffing, phishing, session hijacking, OTP guessing, and man-in-the-middle attacks.
11. Sensitive authentication information shall never be exposed through APIs, logs, notifications, or customer-facing interfaces.
12. Every authentication event shall generate immutable audit records.
13. Authentication records shall remain tamper-resistant.
14. Authentication logs shall support compliance, investigations, and forensic analysis.
15. Fraud Detection Service and Security Monitoring Service shall continuously evaluate authentication behavior.
16. Temporary failures of dependent services shall be handled using retries, circuit breakers, fallback mechanisms, and graceful degradation.
17. Authentication operations shall support distributed tracing through Correlation IDs.
18. Authentication configuration including OTP validity, retry limits, trusted-device duration, and risk policies shall be configurable without application redeployment wherever possible.
19. Operational metrics shall be collected for monitoring, reporting, analytics, observability, capacity planning, and performance optimization.
20. Backup and disaster recovery mechanisms shall protect authentication metadata, configuration, and audit records.
21. Administrative access shall be governed by Role-Based Access Control (RBAC).
22. Customer-facing interfaces shall support localization and accessibility requirements.
23. The module shall comply with organizational security policies, ISO 27001 controls where adopted, OWASP Authentication recommendations, privacy regulations, cybersecurity standards, audit requirements, consumer protection laws where applicable, accessibility standards where applicable, and all applicable legal and regulatory obligations.


#### 3.1.32.14 Acceptance Criteria

1. Only registered and authenticated customers shall be able to configure Two-Factor Authentication settings.
2. Primary authentication shall always be completed before initiating Two-Factor Authentication.
3. Two-Factor Authentication shall be enforced according to configured organizational security policies.
4. Mandatory Two-Factor Authentication shall not be bypassed by customers.
5. Customers shall be able to choose from supported authentication methods when multiple methods are available.
6. Supported authentication methods shall include SMS OTP, Email OTP, Authenticator Application (TOTP), Push Notification Approval, and Backup Recovery Codes where enabled.
7. Every generated OTP shall be cryptographically secure and unique.
8. OTP validity duration shall follow configurable organizational policies.
9. Expired OTPs shall be rejected.
10. Previously used OTPs shall never be accepted.
11. Invalid OTPs shall be rejected.
12. OTP verification attempts shall comply with configured retry limits.
13. OTP resend requests shall comply with configured rate limits.
14. Trusted device authentication shall operate according to configured organizational security policies.
15. High-risk authentication attempts shall always require additional verification regardless of trusted-device status.
16. Fraud Detection Service shall evaluate authentication requests according to configured risk policies.
17. Security Monitoring Service shall continuously evaluate authentication behavior.
18. Successful Two-Factor Authentication shall create an authenticated customer session.
19. Failed authentication shall never create an authenticated session.
20. Authentication tokens shall only be generated after successful Two-Factor Authentication.
21. Refresh tokens shall only be generated after successful authentication where applicable.
22. Login Activity History shall record every successful authentication.
23. Login Activity History shall record every failed authentication.
24. Audit Logging Service shall generate immutable audit records for every authentication event.
25. Device Management Service shall update trusted device information where applicable.
26. Notification Service shall generate authentication notifications according to configured customer preferences and organizational security policies.
27. Administrative operations shall require Role-Based Access Control (RBAC).
28. Administrative activities shall remain fully auditable.
29. Authentication secrets shall never be exposed through customer-facing interfaces.
30. Sensitive authentication data shall never be stored in plaintext.
31. Authentication APIs shall enforce rate limiting and abuse prevention controls.
32. Operational metrics shall be updated for monitoring, reporting, observability, analytics, performance optimization, and capacity planning.
33. Authentication data shall remain synchronized across Authentication Service, Authorization Service, Session Management Service, Device Management Service, Login Activity History Service, Fraud Detection Service, Security Monitoring Service, Customer Profile Service, Notification Service, Analytics Service, and Audit Logging Service.
34. Backup recovery codes shall be single-use only.
35. Regenerating backup recovery codes shall invalidate all previously generated recovery codes.
36. The module shall remain available under expected production workloads without unacceptable degradation.
37. The Two-Factor Authentication module shall comply with organizational security policies, cybersecurity standards, authentication standards, privacy regulations, audit requirements, consumer protection laws where applicable, accessibility standards where applicable, and all applicable legal and regulatory obligations.

---

### 3.1.33 Social Login

#### 3.1.33.1 Description

The Social Login module enables customers to authenticate and access the Food Delivery Platform using trusted third-party identity providers without creating or remembering a separate platform password. The module simplifies customer onboarding, reduces login friction, improves user experience, and accelerates account creation while maintaining enterprise-grade security and compliance.

The platform shall integrate with supported Identity Providers (IdPs) using industry-standard authentication protocols such as OAuth 2.0 and OpenID Connect (OIDC). Authentication shall be delegated to the selected Identity Provider, while the Food Delivery Platform remains responsible for customer authorization, profile management, account linking, session creation, auditing, and compliance.

The platform may support one or more of the following identity providers:

- Google
- Apple ID
- Facebook
- Microsoft
- GitHub
- LinkedIn
- X (Twitter) (Future Enhancement)
- Other enterprise Identity Providers (Future Enhancement)

Customers may use Social Login during:

- New customer registration
- Existing customer login
- Account recovery
- Account linking
- Device migration
- Passwordless authentication
- Cross-platform authentication

If a customer already has an existing Food Delivery Platform account using the same verified email address, the platform may offer secure account linking after successful identity verification according to organizational security policies.

The platform shall never store third-party account passwords. Authentication credentials shall always remain under the responsibility of the Identity Provider.

The module shall integrate with:

- Authentication Service
- Authorization Service
- Identity Provider (IdP)
- Identity Management Service
- Customer Profile Service
- Session Management Service
- Device Management Service
- Login Activity History Service
- Notification Service
- Fraud Detection Service
- Security Monitoring Service
- Analytics Service
- Audit Logging Service

Every successful and failed Social Login attempt shall be securely recorded for security monitoring, auditing, analytics, compliance, and fraud investigation.

---

#### 3.1.33.2 Actors

**Primary Actors**

- Customer
- Registered Customer
- New Customer

**Secondary Actors**

- Authentication Service
- Authorization Service
- Identity Provider (Google, Apple, Facebook, Microsoft, GitHub, LinkedIn, etc.)
- Identity Management Service
- Customer Profile Service
- Session Management Service
- Device Management Service
- Login Activity History Service
- Notification Service
- Fraud Detection Service
- Security Monitoring Service
- Analytics Service
- Audit Logging Service
- System Administrator

---

#### 3.1.33.3 Preconditions

1. Customer shall have internet connectivity.
2. Authentication Service shall be operational.
3. Authorization Service shall be operational.
4. Selected Identity Provider shall be available.
5. OAuth/OpenID Connect integration shall be operational.
6. Platform shall be properly registered with the selected Identity Provider.
7. Valid client credentials shall be configured.
8. Redirect URIs shall be correctly configured.
9. Customer shall possess an active account with the selected Identity Provider.
10. Customer shall successfully authenticate with the selected Identity Provider.
11. Fraud Detection Service shall be operational.
12. Security Monitoring Service shall be operational.
13. Session Management Service shall be operational.
14. Device Management Service shall be operational.
15. Customer Profile Service shall be operational.
16. Audit Logging Service shall be operational.
17. Notification Service shall be operational.
18. Analytics Service shall be operational.
19. Platform security certificates shall be valid.
20. Customer account shall not be suspended where account linking is performed.
21. Identity Provider shall successfully return verified identity claims according to configured scopes.
22. Organizational security policies shall permit Social Login for the selected provider.

---

#### 3.1.33.4 Trigger

The Social Login process may be initiated when one or more of the following events occur:

1. Customer selects **Continue with Google**.
2. Customer selects **Continue with Apple**.
3. Customer selects **Continue with Facebook**.
4. Customer selects **Continue with Microsoft**.
5. Customer selects **Continue with GitHub**.
6. Customer selects **Continue with LinkedIn**.
7. Customer selects another supported Identity Provider.
8. Customer chooses passwordless authentication.
9. Customer attempts faster account registration.
10. Customer logs in from a new device.
11. Customer migrates to another device.
12. Customer requests account recovery using Social Login.
13. Existing customer chooses to link a social account.
14. Organizational authentication policy redirects customers to Social Login.
15. Administrator enables Social Login authentication for the platform.


#### 3.1.33.5 Input Fields

| Field | Description | Mandatory |
|--------|-------------|-----------|
| Identity Provider | Selected Social Login provider | Yes |
| OAuth Authorization Code | Authorization code returned by Identity Provider | Conditional |
| OAuth Access Token | Access token issued by Identity Provider | System Generated |
| ID Token | OpenID Connect identity token | Conditional |
| State Parameter | CSRF protection token | System Generated |
| PKCE Code Verifier | PKCE verification value | Conditional |
| PKCE Code Challenge | PKCE security challenge | System Generated |
| Redirect URI | Configured OAuth callback URI | System Generated |
| Email Address | Verified email received from Identity Provider | Conditional |
| Full Name | Customer full name | Conditional |
| Profile Picture URL | Customer profile image | No |
| Identity Provider User ID | Unique provider user identifier | System Generated |
| Email Verification Status | Indicates whether email is verified | System Generated |
| Device Identifier | Unique customer device identifier | Yes |
| Browser Information | Browser metadata | Yes |
| Operating System | Client operating system | Yes |
| IP Address | Client IP address | System Generated |
| Location Information | Estimated login location | System Generated |
| Correlation ID | Distributed request identifier | System Generated |
| Session ID | Generated authenticated session | System Generated |

---

#### 3.1.33.6 Business Rules

1. Social Login shall use industry-standard OAuth 2.0 and/or OpenID Connect (OIDC) protocols.
2. Only supported Identity Providers shall be accepted.
3. The platform shall never store customer passwords belonging to third-party Identity Providers.
4. Authentication shall always occur at the selected Identity Provider.
5. The platform shall trust only validated identity assertions returned by configured Identity Providers.
6. All identity tokens shall undergo cryptographic signature verification.
7. ID Tokens shall be validated for issuer, audience, expiration, nonce, and signature before authentication succeeds.
8. OAuth authorization codes shall be single-use only.
9. Authorization codes shall expire according to Identity Provider policies.
10. PKCE shall be used for public clients wherever supported.
11. State parameters shall be validated to prevent Cross-Site Request Forgery (CSRF) attacks.
12. Authentication requests shall only originate from registered redirect URIs.
13. Redirect URI mismatches shall immediately reject authentication.
14. Identity claims shall only be accepted from trusted Identity Providers.
15. Customer email addresses received from Identity Providers shall be verified before account linking where required.
16. Existing customer accounts with matching verified email addresses may be linked after successful identity verification.
17. Duplicate customer accounts shall be avoided wherever possible.
18. A single Food Delivery Platform account may support multiple linked social providers according to organizational policy.
19. Customers shall be allowed to unlink social accounts only after satisfying configured authentication requirements.
20. Removing the last authentication method shall not be permitted unless an alternative login mechanism exists.
21. Fraud Detection Service shall evaluate every Social Login request.
